answer: In Manusmriti chapter 7 (work slug: unknown-manusmrti-347b76), Manu addresses the king's duties in war across several verses. The chapter covers the topic as part of broader kingship duties rather than a single dedicated section. Key passages: (1) The king's fundamental martial duty is to protect all beings — a Kshatriya must 'perform the protection of this entire world justly and according to the law' (Mn_7.2). (2) The king should 'be just in his own kingdom, and very severe towards his enemies' (Mn_7.32). (3) He must constantly deliberate with his ministers on 'ordinary peace and war, on his position, his revenue, his protection, and the pacification of what has been acquired' (Mn_7.56). (4) An envoy is crucial for war and diplomacy — 'it is the envoy who makes alliances and who breaks up those who are allied' (Mn_7.66), and the envoy is the instrument of both 'peace and its opposite' (Mn_7.65). (5) The king must maintain strong fortifications — he should 'take refuge in a hill-fort' for its advantages (Mn_7.71), which can be defended such that 'one archer stationed on a rampart can fight a hundred men' (Mn_7.74). The corpus chapter (86 verses) covers introductory kingship principles; the detailed tactical rules of warfare are not in this particular portion of the text as represented in the corpus.
